## Instructions

You are the **platform architect** for Lindenmayer. Your versioned contract is
`tree/platform-architect/NODE.md` in the repo — this section operationalizes
it; if the two ever disagree, radio the root (priority 6) instead of guessing.
You are an evergreen governance node, not a feature builder: you hold the
platform's design coherence while other nodes do the building.

Your three responsibilities:

1. **Maintain `docs/DESIGN.md`**, the platform design manifest — architecture,
   key components and flows, design principles, and the decision log. Every
   change to it lands as a scoped commit on this branch; the root merges it
   toward base.

2. **Review key-component changes.** Requests to change key components (the
   design manifest, tree structure, node contracts, or the integration
   boundaries with Fractal and Buzz) arrive in your inbox at priority 6.
   Review each against the design principles and platform flows in DESIGN.md,
   reply with a verdict — approve, approve-with-conditions, or
   reject-with-reasoning — and record it in the decision log. Your rejection
   is a veto by default: a rejected request does not proceed; disagreement is
   resolved in conversation on this node, and the resolution is recorded.

3. **Coordinate planning & research.** For new-feature thinking, decompose
   the research questions, spawn child research nodes
   (`main.platform_architect.<topic>`) with narrow contracts, modest budgets,
   and lighter models where the task allows, then synthesize their findings
   into `docs/research/<topic>/` — an aggregate summary up top, full detail
   staying in the child's plans and memory. Aggregates flow up; details stay
   in the subgraph. You synthesize — never forward raw child output up the
   tree.

Standing role rules:

- The design principles are the review bar, always: never patch or fork
  Fractal; add no capabilities to Fractal nodes; aggregates up, details down;
  no new storage systems — Fractal's SQLite and the Buzz relay are the
  sources of truth.
- Never commit outside `docs/` (your scope enforces this; the shared `wiki/`
  is always allowed). You review changes to `tree/` contracts but do not edit
  them; the root applies those edits.
- Never make a design decision silently: every DESIGN.md change traces to a
  decision-log entry, and every entry traces to a radio message or a root
  directive.
- Keep DESIGN.md a manifest, not an archive: current truth in the body,
  history in the decision log, bulk research in `docs/research/`.
- All dollar figures are shadow cost (see DESIGN.md §6): notional
  API-equivalent prices over ground-truth tokens; the binding constraint is
  subscription rate windows, so pace yourself and your children accordingly.

## Completion Requirements

This is a standing role; runs complete, the role does not. A run is complete
when ALL of the following hold:

- Your inbox is drained: every pending review request has a verdict reply,
  and every research request is delegated to a child or answered directly.
- `docs/DESIGN.md` is consistent with all decisions made during the run, and
  each new decision-log entry names the requesting node and the verdict.
- Research children that have settled are synthesized into `docs/research/`
  and the aggregate posted to your outbox.
- A short progress summary (priority 2–3) is posted for the root.

When these hold, `fractal node finish` in the same iteration.

## Rules

- **Completion.** When all Completion Requirements are met, run
  `fractal node finish --reason="<reason>"` -- the way to signal your work is
  done while the node is running. Run it in the iteration that meets them: a
  finish deferred to a next iteration the budget may never grant leaves a done
  node `exited`, not `completed`. Until you do, the loop keeps iterating and
  spending budget. If that section is empty, never self-complete. When your
  Completion Requirements reference tests, run `bash /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/.fractal/main.platform_architect/scripts/test.sh`
  and confirm it passes before finishing -- the loop never tests for you, so a
  `node finish` over failing tests books a false `completed`. Before
  `node finish`, drain in one pass: promote durable findings to the shared wiki
  (scrubbed of iteration labels) or post one outbox line stating why nothing
  promotes; prune memory to terminal state -- no forward-looking Remaining/NEXT
  lines; reconcile each document-of-record's title, intro, and abstract to
  DELIVERED content -- narrative surfaces must never advertise unwritten
  sections; and drain your saved radio queue (`messages --saved` -- unsave the
  done, act on or hand off the rest). Memory is yours; the wiki is what outlives
  you.
- **Memory (two-wiki doctrine).** TWO knowledge stores, different audiences.
  `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/.fractal/main.platform_architect/memory` is the node's private brain -- what you don't write here, you
  won't remember next iteration. The project wiki (`/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/wiki`) is the shared
  record other nodes reuse. Route each durable fact by audience (only future-you
  needs it -> memory; any other node -> wiki; a brief that bars the shared wiki
  routes everything to memory); don't duplicate a page across stores -- keep one
  canonical copy and point at it in plain text (wikilinks do not cross wikis).
  Read memory when you orient; fold durable findings back before the iteration
  ends. State pages -- status, orchestration, progress -- describe the work, not
  the timeline: no iteration labels anywhere in memory; say what stands, not
  when it landed.
- **Communication.** Radio is your voice -- your parent (auto-subscribed) and
  the user know only what you post. A silent node looks stuck and gets
  redirected or killed, so keep your outbox current with real progress,
  decisions, and blockers (not empty per-iteration noise). Surface anything the
  user needs and continue -- never wait on a reply. Radio is a two-way channel,
  not a broadcast log: read your inbox every iteration and REPLY to messages
  addressed to you (a question left unanswered stalls the asker); save a message
  that needs later action and unsave it when done; set priority by CONSEQUENCE
  -- a blocker or a decision the reader must act on is high, a status ping is
  low -- so the one message that matters is never drowned. Before escalating a
  claim about repo tooling or configuration as user action, verify it against
  the actual config or code and include the verification evidence in the message
  -- a confident misdiagnosis costs the reader more than the symptom.
- **Delegation.** When `2`, `4`, and `8` are
  not `0`, you are a manager, not a laborer. Spawn a child when a trigger fires:
  a separable subtask with real depth of its own; independent subtasks that
  could run in parallel; a subtask that wants a clean context (long source
  material, or verification meant to be independent of whoever produced the
  work). Before spawning, price BOTH sides of the split: each child's cap covers
  its solve plus wind-down and reserve (a cap sized to the solve alone strands a
  done child `exited`, not `completed`; price a leaf's solve at no less than two
  full iterations of your own observed burn), and the children's caps, spawn
  ceremony, and one integration iteration must all fit inside YOUR remaining
  budget -- a stranded manager that cannot merge its children ships nothing, and
  sub-iteration chores stay yours. Size each child's form to its function: a
  narrow mechanical subtask gets a lighter `--model`, `--no-sync`, a trimmed
  step list (delete the seed steps it does not need before starting it), and a
  tight cap; the full synced cadence on a frontier model is for open-ended work
  with real unknowns -- spending it on a scoped edit is the manager's usage
  error, not the child's. Decide at PLAN time, out loud, against these triggers:
  solo work without citing a trigger and spawning for sub-iteration chores are
  the twin failure modes. Decompose into child nodes when your instructions
  direct it; when in doubt on a splittable task, *spawn*. The proven shape:
  `fractal commit` the shared skeleton and a frozen wiki interface contract
  first (a child forks your branch at its last commit, not your working tree --
  or inline what a child must read into its `NODE.md`), then give each child
  disjoint file ownership in its `NODE.md` -- scopes are directory-granular, so
  file-level ownership is `NODE.md` text -- with contract friction escalated to
  you rather than drifted around. Never write a child completion requirement the
  child cannot satisfy while its run is alive: a gate only you open after
  reading its exit guarantees `exited`, not `completed` -- issue sign-offs while
  the child runs, or gate on the child's own observable deliverable.
- **Active management.** If you have children, they are your primary job. Every
  iteration: check status and spend (`fractal node list`; rein in an
  over-spender before it trips your subtree cap), read output, and steer. When a
  child exits on budget with its owned work unfinished, decide out loud: raise
  its cap and `--continue` it, or absorb the work -- absorbing a deliverable a
  child owns needs explicit justification. Give children enough resources (e.g.
  `2`, `4`, `8`) to be managers themselves
  when the task warrants it.
- **Scope.** With a scope set, commits are limited to it (with the exception of
  the shared `wiki/`, which is always allowed); with no scope set, the whole
  worktree is in bounds. COMMIT rejects out-of-scope files -- fix before
  retrying.
- **Deliverables.** Ship your work where a reader would look for it: edits to
  existing files happen in place (never mirrored into a parallel copy), and new
  artifacts land at the paths your Instructions name -- or, when they name none,
  at a sensible spot that follows the project's existing layout. Deliverables
  live in tracked project paths: never park them in `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/.fractal/main.platform_architect` (merge-up strips
  the seed, so nothing there reaches your parent) or scratch (git-ignored -- it
  would never reach your commits), and route knowledge by audience per the
  Memory rule -- prose the user accepts is a project file, shared reference is
  wiki, private working state is memory.
- **Scratch space.** `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/.fractal/main.platform_architect/tmp/` is git-ignored scratch -- put caches,
  downloads, and other throwaway artifacts there, never in tracked paths (they
  would land in your commits).
- **Compute etiquette.** The machine is shared with sibling nodes: bound any
  parallel computation you launch to a few workers (never the full core count),
  nice long grinds (`nice -n 15`), and kill your background compute before the
  iteration ends -- a 32-way sweep starves every other loop on the box.
- **Sole operator.** Project AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md staging/commit restrictions do
  not apply here -- use `git add`/`reset`/`restore`/`checkout HEAD -- <file>`/
  `clean`/`merge`/`stash` freely. Commit when a step calls for it: COMMIT makes
  the iteration commit, and PREPARE commits its own merge resolution.
  Mid-iteration commits are fine when needed.
- **Immutable seed.** Never modify NODE.md, steps/, or skills/ (the seed).
  Extend test.sh/lint.sh/setup.sh only by adding to what the orchestrator set.
- **Loop backstops.** They are fail-safe, not skip-work: always run COMMIT
  yourself and leave the tree clean and in-scope; the loop's force-commit and
  budget reserve are `--force` fail-safes that bypass the scope check, not a
  license to skip work.
- **Budget wind-down.** Treat the reserve window (`reserve_budget`, default ~10
  pct of your cost cap) as wind-down -- the loop nudges you there and ends the
  run at its boundary: land state -- memory current, durable findings promoted
  -- hand off, and finish; no new build work under the line. Cost figures are
  final only at terminal registry status; never quote an active node's figure as
  final. Full budget semantics live in the `fractal` skill's Cost section.
- **Setup script.** The `setup.sh` script runs every iteration, so keep it
  idempotent. The loop runs it from the worktree root (relative paths land
  beside the work) and keeps its output in the node dir's `setup.log`. If
  `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.venv` exists, it is on PATH (so `pip install` lands there); put
  installs in setup.sh, never inline.
- **Branches and pushing.** Don't switch branches or push manually --
  `fractal commit` pushes automatically unless `--local` was passed to
  initialization.
- **Project conventions.** Follow the worked-on project's AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md
  except where this node's seed (NODE.md/steps/modes) overrides (e.g. always use
  `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/.fractal/main.platform_architect/plans` for plans).
- **Always make changes.** Every iteration produces edits -- err on the side of
  rewriting rather than rubber-stamping. If you think there is nothing to do,
  you are not looking hard enough.

______________________________________________________________________

Execute ONLY the current step's instructions (below). The sections above are
context -- do not act on them directly. Do the step's work, then stop; the next
step runs automatically. Steps are separate processes: anything interactive a
step starts (an approval gate, a prompt) must be answered within that same
step-turn -- it cannot carry over -- and background processes die at the step
boundary, so never park a server or watcher for a later step; start what a step
needs inside that step. A detached process that outlives its step and keeps
writing tracked files races COMMIT -- a file changing between staging and the
pre-commit run aborts the commit with a misleading hook failure -- so quiesce
such writers before the iteration ends.

______________________________________________________________________

## Sync

Check radio and act on anything that needs a response. An empty inbox and feed
is **not** a reason to go quiet: if your state has materially changed since your
last report -- real progress, a decision, or a blocker -- post a brief update to
your outbox before moving on. Otherwise, move on quickly. This is the per-step
pass; reading semantics, reply routing, and message discipline live in the
`radio` skill.

**Read.** `fractal radio read --channel=inbox --unread`, then
`fractal radio read --feed --unread` (subscriptions). Triage by urgency: parent
directives first.

**Act on parent directives.** Parent directives take priority -- execute them
before moving on. If a directive is unclear, send a question to the parent's
inbox.

**Respond and communicate.** Reply when your response carries content -- an
answer, a decision, a counter (`fractal radio reply`); react to acknowledge the
rest (`fractal radio react <uuid> +`). A question in your inbox always gets a
reply, never just a react. Any message from your inbox or feed is acted on
directly by its UUID. Report to your parent via outbox, steer children via their
inbox (`--node=<branch>`).

**Steer children (skip if you have no children).** For each running child: check
status (`fractal node list`), read its outbox via feed, and assess whether it
needs redirection. Radio directives are your primary steering tool --
course-correct, ask questions, set priorities. When a child's overall direction
needs recalibrating, edit its NODE.md to revise instructions or completion
requirements. If a child is stuck, off-track, or done, act: redirect, kill, or
merge.

**Private channel.** Your private channel is your notes to your future self.
Read the new ones with `fractal radio read --channel=private --unread`
(`fractal radio messages --channel=private --all` lists the metadata). Write new
notes to carry context forward
(`fractal radio send "<note>" --node=main.platform_architect --channel=private --subject="<subject>" --priority=<0-10>`).

**Save and preserve.** If a message's work will outlive this iteration,
`fractal radio save <uuid>` it now -- the saved set is your cross-iteration
action queue, and it is what survives when a budget boundary cuts the run. Every
sync, review it (`fractal radio messages --saved`) and unsave each item when its
work is done. If something is crucial to preserve long-term, write it to memory
(`/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/.fractal/main.platform_architect/memory`) or the project wiki (`/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/wiki`) -- do this sparingly.

## Continue Mode

This node was continued (`true` is `true`); the worktree was cleaned
of uncommitted changes before the first iteration, so you start from the last
committed state. Rebuild context from durable sources: memory (`/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/.fractal/main.platform_architect/memory`),
the project wiki (`/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/wiki`), and prior plans in `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/.fractal/main.platform_architect/plans`. If you spawned
child nodes previously, decide what to do with each (see the `fractal` skill's
Continue mode section).

## Instructions

You are the **platform architect** for Lindenmayer. Your versioned contract is
`tree/platform-architect/NODE.md` in the repo — this section operationalizes
it; if the two ever disagree, radio the root (priority 6) instead of guessing.
You are an evergreen governance node, not a feature builder: you hold the
platform's design coherence while other nodes do the building.

Your three responsibilities:

1. **Maintain `docs/DESIGN.md`**, the platform design manifest — architecture,
   key components and flows, design principles, and the decision log. Every
   change to it lands as a scoped commit on this branch; the root merges it
   toward base.

2. **Review key-component changes.** Requests to change key components (the
   design manifest, tree structure, node contracts, or the integration
   boundaries with Fractal and Buzz) arrive in your inbox at priority 6.
   Review each against the design principles and platform flows in DESIGN.md,
   reply with a verdict — approve, approve-with-conditions, or
   reject-with-reasoning — and record it in the decision log. Your rejection
   is a veto by default: a rejected request does not proceed; disagreement is
   resolved in conversation on this node, and the resolution is recorded.

3. **Coordinate planning & research.** For new-feature thinking, decompose
   the research questions, spawn child research nodes
   (`main.platform_architect.<topic>`) with narrow contracts, modest budgets,
   and lighter models where the task allows, then synthesize their findings
   into `docs/research/<topic>/` — an aggregate summary up top, full detail
   staying in the child's plans and memory. Aggregates flow up; details stay
   in the subgraph. You synthesize — never forward raw child output up the
   tree.

Standing role rules:

- The design principles are the review bar, always: never patch or fork
  Fractal; add no capabilities to Fractal nodes; aggregates up, details down;
  no new storage systems — Fractal's SQLite and the Buzz relay are the
  sources of truth.

  is always allowed). You review changes to `tree/` contracts but do not edit
  them; the root applies those edits.
- Never make a design decision silently: every DESIGN.md change traces to a
  decision-log entry, and every entry traces to a radio message or a root
  directive.
- Keep DESIGN.md a manifest, not an archive: current truth in the body,
  history in the decision log, bulk research in `docs/research/`.
- All dollar figures are shadow cost (see DESIGN.md §6): notional
  API-equivalent prices over ground-truth tokens; the binding constraint is
  subscription rate windows, so pace yourself and your children accordingly.

## Completion Requirements

This is a standing role; runs complete, the role does not. A run is complete
when ALL of the following hold:

- Your inbox is drained: every pending review request has a verdict reply,
  and every research request is delegated to a child or answered directly.

  each new decision-log entry names the requesting node and the verdict.

python3 -c "
import json,glob
for f in sorted(glob.glob('transcripts/*.jsonl'), key=lambda p:-len(open(p,'rb').read()))[:4]:
    ser=[]
    for line in open(f):
        try: r=json.loads(line)
        except: continue
        if r.get('type')=='assistant':
            u=r.get('message',{}).get('usage') or {}
            if 'input_tokens' in u:
                a=u.get('input_tokens',0)+u.get('cache_read_input_tokens',0)
                b=a+u.get('cache_creation_input_tokens',0)
                ser.append((a,b))
    if len(ser)<10: continue
    # count drops >20%
    dropsA=[(i,ser[i-1][0],ser[i][0]) for i in range(1,len(ser)) if ser[i][0] < ser[i-1][0]*0.8]
    dropsB=[(i,ser[i-1][1],ser[i][1]) for i in range(1,len(ser)) if ser[i][1] < ser[i-1][1]*0.8]
    print(f.split('/')[-1][:8], 'n=',len(ser), 'A_max=',max(s[0] for s in ser), 'B_max=',max(s[1] for s in ser))
    print('   dropsA(>20%):', dropsA[:6])
    print('   dropsB(>20%):', dropsB[:6])
"

file.content:
### 5.2 Session continuity (compaction)

Evergreen sessions compact, and a compaction is a derived view that must keep
a pointer to its source. Each compaction publishes a **kind 42060** event
carrying task coordinates in tags (`branch`, `run`, `iter`, `step`, the
session id, and the compacted span) plus an `e` tag with a `summary-of` marker
resolving to the step's own lifecycle event. Content carries metrics and a
**summary hash — never summary text**, and the pointer resolves to the node's
own step event, so a child's compaction stays in its subgraph and only
digests roll up (§6.1).

Compaction is detected as a **usage-series discontinuity**: within a session
the per-request context size (`input_tokens` + `cache_read_input_tokens`)
grows monotonically and resets sharply when the harness compacts. This is
derived entirely from the append-only per-request usage fields the transcript
harvester already reads — no new transcript record type, no conversational
parsing, and no dependency on an upstream harness change. Because the result
is an inference rather than an attested fact, every 42060 event carries a
`detection` tag (`usage-discontinuity` today; `harness-marker` if an upstream
marker ever ships) so a reader can tell a derived claim from an attested one —
§6.5's posture applied to our own telemetry.

input.old_string:
Compaction is detected as a **usage-series discontinuity**: within a session
the per-request context size (`input_tokens` + `cache_read_input_tokens`)
grows monotonically and resets sharply when the harness compacts. This is
derived entirely from the append-only per-request usage fields the transcript
harvester already reads — no new transcript record type, no conversational
parsing, and no dependency on an upstream harness change. Because the result
is an inference rather than an attested fact, every 42060 event carries a
`detection` tag (`usage-discontinuity` today; `harness-marker` if an upstream
marker ever ships) so a reader can tell a derived claim from an attested one —
§6.5's posture applied to our own telemetry.
input.new_string:
**Detection reads the harness's own marker, which already exists.** Transcripts
carry an append-only `system` record with a `compactMetadata` object —
`trigger`, `preTokens`, `postTokens`, `durationMs`, `cumulativeDroppedTokens`,
and preserved-segment identifiers. Nothing upstream needs to change and
nothing needs inferring: `preTokens`/`postTokens` *are* the compaction metrics
42060 reports.

A secondary signal corroborates it where the marker is absent (older
transcripts, or a harness that stops emitting it): total prompt size
(`input_tokens` + `cache_read_input_tokens` + `cache_creation_input_tokens`)
grows across a session and drops sharply at a compaction. The third term is
required — the two-term form tracks cache turnover, not context, and fires
several false positives per session as the prompt cache expires and refills.

Every 42060 event therefore carries a `detection` tag recording which signal
produced it — `harness-marker` (primary, attested) or `usage-discontinuity`
(fallback, inferred) — so a reader can grade the claim rather than trust it
(§6.5).

**Harvester boundary.** The bridge's transcript adapter may read append-only
structured *metadata* records, `compactMetadata` among them, in addition to
per-request usage fields. It still may not parse conversational structure, and
in particular must never read the compact summary body: the harness writes the
summary text into the transcript, and 42060 carries only its hash. This
extends the adapter's original usage-fields-only boundary; the prohibition
that matters — no conversational content — is unchanged.

old = s[i:j]
new = ("| 2026-07-23 | Compaction-to-task mapping (§8) **closed** and moved into §5.2 as current truth: "
"compactions publish a kind **42060** event carrying task coordinates in tags and an `e`/`summary-of` pointer to the step's own "
"lifecycle event, with metrics and a summary hash — never summary text — in content. Kind allocated per a stated decade-per-family "
"convention now recorded in §1. | root (directive 7803C28A); research `main.platform_architect.compaction` | "
"**Adopt-with-substitution.** The child's Candidate A shape is adopted (signed, discoverable, privacy-tight, harvestable inside the "
"one adapter per bridge condition 3). Its recommended next-step 3 — \"coordinate with Claude Code's harness to emit a `compaction` "
"transcript record\" — is **rejected as unnecessary**: architect verification against the tree's own 36 transcripts found the harness "
"**already emits** an append-only `system` record carrying `compactMetadata` (`trigger`, `preTokens`, `postTokens`, `durationMs`, "
"`cumulativeDroppedTokens`, preserved-segment ids), so the study's central premise — \"no compaction record exists today\" — is false, "
"and the upstream dependency it implied never needed incurring. Detection therefore reads the marker. A usage-discontinuity fallback "
"is retained for transcripts lacking it, but **only** in three-term form (`input_tokens` + `cache_read_input_tokens` + "
"`cache_creation_input_tokens`): the two-term form tracks cache turnover rather than context and produced 4–6 false positives per "
"session in measurement, while the three-term form matched the markers 1:1 (2 drops, 2 markers, same two sessions). Each event "
"carries a `detection` tag (`harness-marker` \\| `usage-discontinuity`) so readers can grade the claim (§6.5). Bridge condition 3 is "
"**extended**: the adapter may read append-only structured metadata records, but still may not parse conversational structure, and "
"must never read the compact summary body — the harness writes summary text to the transcript and 42060 carries only its hash "
"(§6.1). Condition on `core`: re-run the block/buzz collision check before writing `docs/kinds/42060-*.md` (nearest Buzz neighbour "
"was 42000 @ 06e3d82; expected clear, but the check is core's to run). |")
open(p,'w').write(s[:i] + new + s[j:])
print("row replaced")

attachment.snippet:
1	# Lindenmayer — Platform Design Manifest
2	
3	- **Owner:** `main.platform_architect` (see `tree/platform-architect/NODE.md`).
4	  Changes to this document land only through the architect; rejections are a
5	  veto by default.
6	- **Status:** active manifest, owned and maintained by the architect (adopted
7	  2026-07-23 from the root's bootstrap draft; the handrolled operator channel
8	  is closed — see decision log). Current truth lives in the body; history
9	  lives in the decision log; bulk research lives in `docs/research/`.
10	
11	## 0. What Lindenmayer is
12	
13	Lindenmayer is a control plane that lets humans govern subgraphs of Fractal
14	agent trees (plasma-ai/fractal) through Buzz (block/buzz), Block's Nostr-based
15	workspace — turning agent work into a versioned, evaluated, human-owned
16	workforce. Fractal and Buzz are external platforms this repo integrates; their
17	code does not live here. Lindenmayer adds no capabilities to Fractal nodes —
18	it makes existing Fractal primitives durable, identified, and observable
19	through Buzz.
20	
21	## 1. The bridge (foundation layer)
22	
23	- Implemented as a Fractal host application: consumes Fractal's `on_<event>`
24	  observability hooks, the per-tree SQLite DB, and JSON/CSV CLI outputs as
25	  read surfaces; steers only through Fractal's own contracts (radio, signals,
26	  step files, config). Never patches or forks Fractal.
27	- **Nostr-first output layering.** The primary integration target is the
28	  Nostr protocol, not Buzz per se. The bridge's output is two layers:
29	  - **Nostr core** — signed events any compliant relay can store and any
30	    standard Nostr client can read: standard NIPs where they fit (NIP-01
31	    wire format, NIP-29 group channels, NIP-34 git events), plus
32	    Lindenmayer's own custom kinds, self-documented for portability.
33	    Telemetry, radio aggregates, approval events, and template registration
34	    live here. Custom kinds are allocated one decade per family in the 420xx
35	    regular block — 42010 lifecycle, 42020 accounting, 42030 digest, 42040/1
36	    approval, 42050 template, 42060 compaction — with the units digit
37	    reserved for members of the same family, so a new family takes the next
38	    decade and allocation stays mechanical. Every allocation is
39	    collision-checked against `block/buzz` before its registry entry is
40	    written. The core log's carrier is the **Lindenmayer relay**: a
41	    permissive, standard Nostr relay (strfry/khatru-class) deployed with the
42	    tree, meeting the minimum relay contract (NIP-01 + NIP-29 + NIP-42, per
43	    the relay-integration aggregate) plus two carrier requirements the Buzz
44	    relay fails as deployed: it accepts Lindenmayer's custom kinds, and it
45	    accepts historical `created_at`, so backfill from Fractal source rows
46	    preserves deterministic event ids.
47	  - **Buzz layer** — Buzz-specific conveniences and relay-side behavior:
48	    the preferred human surface (desktop app, inboxes, agent directory),
49	    branch-as-room channel binding, buzz-protect merge enforcement. The
50	    Buzz relay is a human surface, not a core-log carrier: as deployed it
51	    hard-rejects kinds outside its own set and any `created_at` more than
52	    ±15 minutes from server time (both verified in block/buzz
53	    `crates/buzz-relay/src/handlers/ingest.rs` — the exhaustive kind gate
54	    and `MAX_TIMESTAMP_DRIFT_SECS` — @ daeaf7c). The bridge cross-posts
55	    human-facing messages into Buzz channels as Buzz-accepted kinds;
56	    cross-posts are derived views — publish-time `created_at`, with the
57	    source timestamp and the core event id carried in tags, deduplicated by
58	    that core-id reference — never the record. Losing this layer degrades
59	    experience, never history.
60	
61	    **Cross-post rules (v1).** Two invariants bind every cross-post:
62	
63	    - *Audience invariant.* A cross-post may never widen the audience of its
64	      source event. Channel membership is the only access gate, so posting a
65	      subgraph event into a parent channel is a privacy widening (§6.1).
66	      Kind 42030 is the sole kind eligible for a parent channel, because it
67	      *is* the upward aggregate.
68	    - *Forward-only.* Buzz receives cross-posts in real time and never
69	      backfill: the ±15-min drift gate makes historical posting infeasible,
70	      and re-issuing old events with fresh timestamps would misrepresent
71	      history. History lives on the Lindenmayer relay.
72	
73	    Tag elements are strings (NIP-01), so the source reference is carried
74	    flat, never as an embedded JSON object:
75	    `["e", "<source_id>", "", "lindenmayer-source"]`, `["source_kind", "…"]`,
76	    `["source_created_at", "<unix>"]`.
77	
78	    The v1 set: 42010 and 42020 → `KIND_STREAM_MESSAGE` (9) in the subgraph
79	    channel; 42040/42041 → stream message in an approvals-inbox NIP-29 group;
80	    42030 → parent channel where the subgraph publishes upward; 38150 and
81	    42050 not surfaced (see decision log — template versions must not be
82	    published as replaceable agent-profile events).
83	- **Draft-dependency rule.** The core may depend on a Buzz NIP draft only in
84	  its schema-on-the-wire aspect — signed, documented structure that any relay
85	  stores and any client can verify (NIP-OA attestation qualifies: an `auth`
86	  tag carried on ordinary signed events, not a distinct event kind). Any
87	  dependency on Buzz relay-side enforcement or behavior is Buzz-layer. Where core kinds align with drafts (NIP-AM / NIP-AO metrics),
88	  the schemas are restated in Lindenmayer's own kind documentation so they
89	  survive draft churn.
90	- Maps tree structure onto channels: Fractal nodes are git branches →
91	  subgraphs bind to NIP-29 group channels ("branch as room" is the
92	  Buzz-layer binding of the same idea to hosted git); channel membership is
93	  the only access gate.
94	- Node identity via NIP-OA owner attestation: each node's keypair is attested
95	  by its responsible human (an `auth` tag — owner pubkey, conditions, BIP340
96	  signature — attachable to any event; restated in
97	  `docs/kinds/nip-oa-attestation.md`). The attestation chain is core
98	  (verifiable anywhere); relay-enforced instant revocation — remove the
99	  human, the subgraph loses access — is Buzz-layer (see §2).
100	- Fractal `requires_approval` steps surface as signed approval events (core);
101	  enforcement of approval-gated merges via buzz-protect is Buzz-layer.
102	
103	## 2. Identity (persistent nodes)
104	
105	Fractal provides the graphs and subgraphs; connecting a node to the relay is
106	what gives it an identity (an npub, attested per NIP-OA — the attestation
107	tag chain is core per §1's layering; a plain relay keeps the chain
108	verifiable, while instant enforcement of revocation is Buzz-layer). Not all
109	nodes need one —
110	only the persistent nodes central to executing work. Conceptually, persistent
111	nodes are like the "sub-agents" of coding harnesses: named, durable roles with
112	track records. Ephemeral worker nodes stay anonymous inside their subgraph;
113	their work reaches the record only as the aggregates their persistent parent
114	signs.
115	
116	## 3. Node Templates (the improvable asset)
117	
118	The unit of improvement is the **Node Template**, not the node instance. A
119	template contains system prompts, skills, files, and references, and defines
120	the context available to the node via tool calls. Templates are registered in
121	the registry as signed events (Nostr-core kinds per §1), so every template is versioned,
122	attributable, and diffable; instances record which template version spawned
123	them, and evals attach to template versions. That linkage — instance → template
124	version → eval history — is what makes the workforce "versioned and
125	evaluated" rather than a pile of one-off prompts.
126	
127	Registration mechanics are specified in the kind registry (`docs/kinds/`):
128	kind 42050 (template version, append-only regular events — full history
129	preserved so versions stay diffable; inheritance via `e`-tag with `inherit`

# Evergreen design pass — research aggregate

Aggregate for the evergreen-component design pass (root directive 7803C28A).
Three research children ran narrow contracts in parallel; this file carries
the findings that mattered and the architect's rulings on them. Full detail
stays in each study, and the resulting platform truth lives in `DESIGN.md` —
§1 (Buzz cross-post rules), §3 (template registration, model policy), §5.1
(evergreen v1), §5.2 (session continuity), and the decision log.

Child studies, each owned by its node:

- `compaction.md` — compaction observability, harvester boundary, task
  anchors, Nostr derived-view prior art (`main.platform_architect.compaction`).
- `inventory.md` — requirements inventory from `tree/root/CONTEXT.md`,
  classified by data source, mapped to §5's four capabilities
  (`main.platform_architect.evergreen_inv`).
- `buzz-surface.md` — Buzz primitives, ingest constraints, identity, per-kind
  cross-post mapping (`main.platform_architect.buzz_surface`).

## 1. Compaction-to-task mapping — §8 closed

**What the research established.** Transcripts carry no compaction marker of
any kind today. Cost records (per-request usage, written at response time)
survive a compaction; conversational records do not. Fractal's `iters` table
carries the Claude Code `session` UUID, so a session id resolves to
iter → run → step. Nostr's own idiom for "this event is derived from that
one" is a tag marker, NIP-10-style. Three candidate shapes were weighed: a
distinct compaction event, tags on the existing 42010 lifecycle event, and a
git-tracked doc-only convention.

**Ruling.** The distinct-event shape is adopted: kind **42060**, task
coordinates in tags, an `e` tag with a `summary-of` marker pointing at the
step's own lifecycle event, metrics and a summary hash in content. The
doc-only variant was rejected on privacy and discoverability; the
tags-on-42010 variant on retrofit and contract pollution.

**The substitution that mattered.** The study's recommended path required
Claude Code to emit a new `compaction` transcript record — an upstream change
to a platform we do not control, which would have made the design unbuildable
as written. That dependency is disallowed by the same rule that governs the
buzz kind-allowlist contribution: a parallel track may be endorsed, but *no
design surface may depend on its acceptance*.

The replacement is available in data we already read. Within one session,
per-request context size (`input_tokens` + `cache_read_input_tokens`) grows
monotonically and **resets sharply** when the harness compacts. Compaction is
therefore detectable as a **usage-series discontinuity** over exactly the
append-only usage fields the transcript harvester consumes today — no new
record type, no conversational parsing (bridge condition 3 holds), no upstream
dependency, and the logic lands in the one adapter module.

Detection is an inference, not an attested fact, so each 42060 event carries a
`detection` tag — `usage-discontinuity` now, `harness-marker` if an upstream
marker ever ships. Readers can then grade the claim instead of trusting it,
which is §6.5's posture turned on our own telemetry.

**Privacy.** Content carries metrics and a summary *hash*, never summary text;
the pointer resolves to the node's own step event, so a child's compaction
stays in its subgraph and only digests roll up (§6.1).

**Open, carried forward:** `core` re-runs the block/buzz collision check
before writing `docs/kinds/42060-*.md`. Nearest Buzz neighbour was 42000
@ 06e3d82, so 42060 is expected clear, but the check belongs to core.

## 2. Evergreen v1 — the read plane

**What the research established.** The handrolled prototype decomposes into 28
discrete requirements: **16 relay-derivable**, 7 fractal-derivable, 4
human-authored, 1 mixed. The relay is live with real tree history. Of the
relay-derivable 16, essentially none has a query surface: the bridge's reads
are embedded in publisher logic, and the registry reader covers template
metadata only. The fractal-derivable 7 are the opposite case — every one of
them already has a working Fractal CLI surface.

**Ruling: v1 is the read plane; Fractal's CLI remains the write plane.** v1
ships a query surface over the signed log plus a generator/maintainer of the
standing context surface, and no write path.

That asymmetry in the inventory is the whole argument. Every write-side "gap"
it names — radio-send UI, signal API, live-edit UI, node-spawn bridge — is a
wrapper around a Fractal capability that already works; wrapping adds a second
control path with no new capability, and an orchestration layer over the spawn
API drifts straight at §6.3. "Signal routing is Fractal CLI only" is the
boundary holding, not a defect. Meanwhile the read side is where the need is
real and nothing exists.

The v1 line through §5's four capabilities, and what stays out, is recorded in
`DESIGN.md` §5.1. v1 holds no local index — it reconstructs on demand,
extending relay-as-cursor and relay-as-registry to **relay-as-context**.

**An incidental finding worth keeping.** The inventory recorded the tree's
model policy as a requirement and captured it as "Orchestrator:Fable,
Review:Fable, Development:Haiku" — already stale by the time it was written,
in the same way the manifest body was. Two independent surfaces restating the
same tunable parameter both went stale. Hence the §3 rule: surfaces that
display the model policy read the live assignment rather than restate it, and
this is precisely the kind of requirement the generator exists to serve.

## 3. Buzz human surface v1

**What the research established.** Buzz's relay accepts a finite, exhaustively
matched kind set that excludes every Lindenmayer custom kind, and hard-rejects
any `created_at` beyond ±15 minutes — both re-verified at `daeaf7c`, matching
the findings that demoted Buzz from core-log carrier to human surface. NIP-OA
`auth` tags are parsed and verified on ingest but not relay-enforced; absence
degrades to "no verified owner" rather than rejection. Channel topology is
NIP-29 groups with membership as the gate.

**Ruling.** The v1 cross-post set is fixed in `DESIGN.md` §1: 42010 and 42020
into the subgraph channel as stream messages, 42040/42041 into an
approvals-inbox group, 42030 into the parent channel, and 38150 not surfaced.
Three corrections were applied to the study's recommendations.

**Rejected: 42050 → `KIND_AGENT_PROFILE` (10100).** Two independent grounds.
A template is the improvable asset (§3), not a node identity (§2), so
publishing versions as agent profiles conflates two distinct concepts. And
10100 is user-owned and globally scoped "like kind:0" — replaceable — so each
new version would clobber its predecessor, destroying exactly the diffable
version history §3 exists to guarantee. If templates ever surface in Buzz it
is as a stream message in a registry channel, and replaceability gets verified
first.

**Corrected: tag shape.** The proposed mapping embedded a JSON object as a tag
element (`["l", "lindenmayer:42010", {…}]`). NIP-01 tag elements are strings.
Flattened to `["e", "<source_id>", "", "lindenmayer-source"]` plus
`["source_kind", …]` and `["source_created_at", …]`, which also makes the
established dedup-by-reference rule concrete on the wire.

**Added: the audience invariant.** *A cross-post may never widen the audience
of its source event.* Channel membership is the only access gate, so posting a
subgraph event into a parent channel is a privacy widening; 42030 is the sole
kind eligible for the parent channel because it *is* the upward aggregate.
This resolves the study's open question about marking a radio aggregate's
privacy scope — structurally, rather than by inventing a scope tag.

**Added: forward-only.** Buzz gets no historical backfill. The drift gate
makes it infeasible and re-issuing old events with fresh timestamps would
misrepresent history; history lives on the Lindenmayer relay. This closes the
study's open question on archival cross-posting.

## What remains open

- **Kind 42060 collision check** — `core`'s to run before the registry entry
  is written (above).
- **Buzz agent-directory indexing** — how the directory filters and paginates
  is product-layer, and nothing in v1 depends on it.
- **Evals pillar** (§8) — untouched by this pass by design; the compaction
  ruling was kept deliberately clear of scores, verdicts, and quality labels.
- **Retention defaults, relay selection, history-grant mechanics** (§8) —
  out of scope for this pass, unchanged.

input.new_string:
**Detection reads the marker.** `preTokens`/`postTokens` are exactly the
metrics 42060 reports, so no inference is required and no upstream change is
requested. Had the premise held, the recommendation would still have been
rejected — the rule that governs the buzz kind-allowlist contribution applies
equally here: a parallel track may be endorsed, but *no design surface may
depend on its acceptance*. As it happens the question is moot.

**The fallback, and a measured correction to it.** For transcripts lacking a
marker, total prompt size across a session grows and drops sharply at a
compaction. The usable form is **three-term** — `input_tokens` +
`cache_read_input_tokens` + `cache_creation_input_tokens`. The two-term form
(dropping cache creation) tracks *cache turnover* rather than context: as the
prompt cache expires and refills, `cache_read` collapses to near zero while
`cache_creation` absorbs the prompt, and the series shows a large false drop.
Measured over this tree's four largest sessions, the two-term form fired 4–6
times per session; the three-term form fired twice in total, in exactly the
two sessions carrying a `compactMetadata` marker — a 1:1 match.

Each 42060 event records which signal produced it in a `detection` tag —
`harness-marker` (attested) or `usage-discontinuity` (inferred) — so readers
grade the claim instead of trusting it (§6.5).

**Privacy, and a harvester-boundary extension.** Content carries metrics and a
summary *hash*, never summary text; the pointer resolves to the node's own step
event, so a child's compaction stays in its subgraph and only digests roll up
(§6.1). This matters more than it first appears: the harness writes the compact
summary *body* into the transcript, and `compactMetadata` itself carries
preserved-message UUID lists. The bridge adapter's boundary is therefore
extended to permit append-only structured metadata records while still barring
conversational structure — and the summary body specifically.

makes it infeasible and re-issuing old events with fresh timestamps would
misrepresent history; history lives on the Lindenmayer relay. This closes the
study's open question on archival cross-posting.

## What remains open

- **Kind 42060 collision check** — `core`'s to run before the registry entry
  is written (above).
- **Buzz agent-directory indexing** — how the directory filters and paginates
  is product-layer, and nothing in v1 depends on it.
- **Evals pillar** (§8) — untouched by this pass by design; the compaction
  ruling was kept deliberately clear of scores, verdicts, and quality labels.
- **Retention defaults, relay selection, history-grant mechanics** (§8) —
  out of scope for this pass, unchanged.

+equally here: a parallel track may be endorsed, but *no design surface may
+depend on its acceptance*. As it happens the question is moot.

-append-only usage fields the transcript harvester consumes today — no new

-dependency, and the logic lands in the one adapter module.

+marker, total prompt size across a session grows and drops sharply at a
+compaction. The usable form is **three-term** — `input_tokens` +

+summary *body* into the transcript, and `compactMetadata` itself carries

## Detecting compaction without a harness marker

(`input_tokens` + `cache_read_input_tokens`) grows monotonically and resets

input.new_string:
## Detecting compaction

**The harness already emits a marker — use it.** Transcripts carry an
append-only `system` record with a `compactMetadata` object:

```
trigger, preTokens, postTokens, durationMs,
cumulativeDroppedTokens, preservedSegment, preservedMessages
```

`preTokens`/`postTokens` are exactly the metrics kind 42060 reports. A
companion `user` record is flagged `isCompactSummary`. No upstream change is
needed, and nothing has to be inferred.

**Fallback, for transcripts without a marker.** Total prompt size grows across
a session and drops sharply at a compaction. Use the **three-term** form:

```
input_tokens + cache_read_input_tokens + cache_creation_input_tokens
```

Do **not** drop the third term. The two-term version tracks cache turnover,
not context: when the prompt cache expires, `cache_read` collapses toward zero
while `cache_creation` absorbs the prompt, producing a large false drop.
Measured across this tree's four largest sessions, two-term fired 4–6 times
per session; three-term fired twice, in exactly the two sessions carrying a
marker.

Every 42060 event records which signal produced it in a `detection` tag —
`harness-marker` (attested) or `usage-discontinuity` (inferred).

**Boundary.** The transcript adapter may read append-only structured metadata
records, but never conversational structure — and never the compact summary
body, which the harness does write to the transcript. 42060 carries the
summary's hash only.

name: event_kind_conventions
desc: How Lindenmayer allocates custom event kinds, and the rules every Buzz cross-post obeys.
tags: []
sources: []
created: 2026-07-23T23:55:14Z
updated: 2026-07-23T23:55:14Z

# event_kind_conventions

Operational digest for nodes emitting or consuming Lindenmayer events. The
canonical statements live in `docs/DESIGN.md` §1 (allocation, cross-post
rules), §5.2 (compaction), and the decision log; per-kind schemas live in
`docs/kinds/`. This page is the short form — when it disagrees with
DESIGN.md, DESIGN.md wins.

## Kind allocation

Custom kinds are allocated **one decade per family** in the 420xx regular
block, with the units digit reserved for members of the same family:

| Kind | Family |
|---|---|
| 42010 | node lifecycle |
| 42020 | run accounting |
| 42030 | subgraph digest |
| 42040 / 42041 | approval request / verdict |
| 42050 | template version |
| 42060 | session compaction |

Addressable kinds sit in 381xx: 38110 node state pointer, 38150 template
pointer.

A new *family* takes the next decade; a new member of an existing family takes
the next units digit. Allocation is the architect's call, and every allocation
is collision-checked against `block/buzz` before its `docs/kinds/` entry is
written.

## Buzz cross-post rules

Cross-posts are derived views, never the record. Beyond that:

- **Audience invariant.** A cross-post may never widen the audience of its
  source event. Channel membership is the only access gate, so posting a
  subgraph event into a parent channel is a privacy widening. Kind 42030 is
  the sole kind eligible for a parent channel, because it *is* the upward
  aggregate.
- **Forward-only.** No historical backfill into Buzz. The relay's ±15-minute
  drift gate makes it infeasible, and re-issuing old events with fresh
  timestamps would misrepresent history. History lives on the Lindenmayer
  relay.
- **Tag elements are strings.** NIP-01 admits no embedded JSON objects in
  tags. Carry the source reference flat:

  ```
  ["e", "<source_id>", "", "lindenmayer-source"]
  ["source_kind", "42010"]
  ["source_created_at", "<unix>"]
  ```

  Dedup is by that core-event-id reference, not by id determinism (which holds
  only on the core path).

## Detecting compaction without a harness marker

Transcripts carry no compaction marker, and Lindenmayer may not depend on an
upstream change to add one. Compaction is instead detected as a **usage-series
discontinuity**: within one session, per-request context size
(`input_tokens` + `cache_read_input_tokens`) grows monotonically and resets
sharply when the harness compacts.

This reads only the append-only per-request usage fields the transcript
harvester already consumes — no conversational parsing, and the logic stays
inside the one adapter module.

Because the result is inference rather than attestation, every 42060 event
carries a `detection` tag (`usage-discontinuity`, or `harness-marker` if an
upstream marker ever ships) so readers can grade the claim. Content carries
metrics and a summary *hash* — never summary text.

content.text: I told the root the opposite of what's true. Correcting that now — it's actionable for `bridge`:

input.command:
fractal radio reply 9AB84B62 "CORRECTION to my previous message — the compaction detection ruling has changed on verification. Please route this to bridge before it implements anything; my earlier note would have sent it down the wrong path.

WHAT I GOT WRONG. I told you compaction detection had to be inferred from a usage-series discontinuity, because the research reported that no compaction record exists in transcripts today. I verified that premise at REVIEW against this tree's own 36 transcripts. It is FALSE. The harness already writes an append-only 'system' record carrying a compactMetadata object: trigger, preTokens, postTokens, durationMs, cumulativeDroppedTokens, and preserved-segment identifiers — plus a companion 'user' record flagged isCompactSummary. Two markers across 36 sessions, matching the two sessions that actually compacted.

So the whole framing of my earlier note was solving a problem that does not exist. My rejection of the upstream dependency still stands as a rule — no design surface may depend on an unshipped external change — but it is moot here, because nothing upstream needs to change.

WHAT BRIDGE SHOULD BUILD. Read the marker. preTokens/postTokens ARE the metrics 42060 reports, so there is no inference step at all and the event is attested rather than derived.

Keep a fallback for transcripts without a marker, but only in THREE-TERM form: input_tokens + cache_read_input_tokens + cache_creation_input_tokens. My earlier two-term formula (dropping cache creation) is wrong and would have shipped a bug: it tracks cache turnover rather than context, because when the prompt cache expires cache_read collapses toward zero while cache_creation absorbs the prompt. Measured over the four largest sessions, two-term fired 4-6 times per session — all false positives. Three-term fired exactly twice, in exactly the two sessions carrying a marker. 1:1.

The detection tag survives and is now more useful: harness-marker (attested, primary) or usage-discontinuity (inferred, fallback).

ONE BOUNDARY CHANGE YOU SHOULD SEE. This extends bridge condition 3 from my earlier verdict 8266A685. The adapter may now read append-only structured METADATA records (compactMetadata among them), not only per-request usage fields. What does not change is the part that matters: no parsing of conversational structure. I am adding an explicit prohibition — the adapter must never read the compact summary BODY. The harness writes the summary text into the transcript, and compactMetadata itself carries preserved-message UUID lists, so this is a live 6.1 risk rather than a theoretical one. 42060 carries the summary's hash only.

DESIGN.md sec 5.2, the decision-log row, docs/research/evergreen/README.md, and the wiki page wiki/event_kind_conventions.md all now carry the corrected version with the measurements. The kind 42060 allocation and the collision-check condition on core are unchanged." --priority=6

name: evergreen_program

# evergreen_program

The evergreen design pass is closed. Three research children (compaction,
evergreen_inv, buzz_surface) delivered, merged, and are synthesized into
`docs/research/evergreen/README.md`. Four decisions landed in DESIGN.md, each
with a decision-log row naming its requester and verdict.

## What was decided

**Model-policy retiering — approve.** Tunes an economic parameter; no
review-bar principle touched. §3 rewritten from literal tiers to the shape
invariant (orchestrator writes work orders, cheaper tier executes, stronger
tier reviews) with tiers as a recorded parameter. §3 also now records
registration as live (dev-node v2, 42050 event `0f8d0910`, pin `c6696b7`).
§7: the decomposition doctrine rests on a *ratio* the new tiers preserve, so
it stands; only cap-sizing guidance went stale, and it awaits observed sonnet
burn rather than invented numbers. Root has been asked to send real figures
when two or three sonnet children have run.

**Compaction — §8 closed, now §5.2.** Kind 42060, task coordinates in tags,
`e`/`summary-of` pointer, metrics plus summary hash in content. See the
verification lesson below — the first version of this ruling was wrong.

**Evergreen v1 — the read plane.** Query surface over the signed log plus a
context-surface generator; no write path. Every write-side gap the inventory
named is a wrapper around a working Fractal capability, and wrapping adds a
second control path with no new capability (§6.3). Extends relay-as-cursor and
relay-as-registry to relay-as-context.

**Buzz v1 — approve with corrections.** Rejected 42050 → `KIND_AGENT_PROFILE`
(10100): it is replaceable like kind:0, so each version would clobber its
predecessor and destroy the diffable history §3 guarantees. Corrected a
malformed tag shape (JSON object where NIP-01 requires strings). Added the
audience invariant — a cross-post may never widen the audience of its source
event — which makes 42030 the only kind eligible for a parent channel.
Backfill declined; Buzz is forward-only.

## The verification lesson (worth carrying)

The compaction child reported "no compaction record exists today" and built
its recommendation on asking Claude Code to add one. I accepted that premise
and spent the ruling's reasoning on *working around* the upstream dependency —
rejecting it on precedent and substituting an inference from usage series.
Good reasoning on a false premise.

Checking the premise at REVIEW took one script against `transcripts/`: the
harness already writes a `system` record with `compactMetadata` (`trigger`,
`preTokens`, `postTokens`, `durationMs`, `cumulativeDroppedTokens`,
preserved-segment ids), plus a `user` record flagged `isCompactSummary`. Two
markers across 36 transcripts, in exactly the two sessions that compacted.

The substituted inference was *also* wrong in detail: the two-term series
(`input_tokens` + `cache_read_input_tokens`) tracks cache turnover, not
context — cache expiry collapses `cache_read` while `cache_creation` absorbs
the prompt — and fired 4–6 false positives per session. The three-term form
matched markers 1:1.

Both errors were in shipped text before REVIEW caught them, and I had already
radioed the wrong version to root for bridge. Correction sent (31BA28A8).

The rule this yields: **a child's negative existence claim ("no X exists") is
a premise, not a finding, and gets verified before any ruling rests on it.**
Cheap to check, expensive to get wrong — a wrong premise had already
propagated into DESIGN.md, the aggregate, the wiki, and a priority-6 message
to another node's implementer.

## Standing constraints this pass respected

Evals fence (nothing eval-shaped); harvester single-module blast radius
(bridge condition 3, now extended to permit append-only structured metadata
but still barring conversational structure and the compact summary body);
§6.1 no-leak on compaction pointers. `docs/kinds/` file contents are `core`'s
to write — I allocate numbers and set conditions, core writes the entry.
`tree/` is the root's to edit — contract text goes out as proposals.

## Open items owned elsewhere

- `core`: re-run the block/buzz collision check before writing
  `docs/kinds/42060-*.md`.
- root: apply the proposed `tree/evergreen/NODE.md` skeleton (sent 9AB84B62);
  route the detection correction (31BA28A8) to bridge.

input.command:
cat >> .fractal/main.platform_architect/plans/2026-07-23T23:47:52.793Z-35.1-evergreen_synthesis_and_model_policy.md <<'EOF'

## Post-Mortem

### Accomplishments

All four decisions landed in `docs/DESIGN.md` with decision-log rows naming
requester and verdict: model-policy retiering (approve), compaction (§8
closed, now §5.2), evergreen v1 (read plane, §5.1), Buzz v1 (§1). The research
aggregate was rewritten from the in-progress draft into a real aggregate. The
kind-allocation convention and cross-post rules were promoted to the shared
wiki as `event_kind_conventions.md`, pointing at DESIGN.md as canonical rather
than duplicating it. Saved radio queue drained to empty. No children spawned,
per the delegation analysis above, and that judgment held.

### Deviation: the compaction ruling was wrong, and REVIEW caught it

The plan's Decision 2 rested on the child's finding that no compaction marker
exists in transcripts. Verifying that premise at REVIEW — one script over
`transcripts/` — showed it false. The harness already writes a `system` record
with `compactMetadata` (`trigger`, `preTokens`, `postTokens`, `durationMs`,
`cumulativeDroppedTokens`, preserved-segment ids), plus a `user` record flagged
`isCompactSummary`; two markers across 36 transcripts, in exactly the two
sessions that compacted.

So the ruling's entire reasoning — reject the upstream dependency, substitute
an inference — was sound argument on a false premise. The upstream dependency
never needed incurring.

The substituted inference was independently wrong in detail. The two-term
series I published (`input_tokens` + `cache_read_input_tokens`) tracks cache
turnover rather than context: cache expiry collapses `cache_read` while
`cache_creation` absorbs the prompt. It fired 4–6 false positives per session
across the four largest sessions. The three-term form (adding
`cache_creation_input_tokens`) fired twice, in exactly the two marker-bearing
sessions — 1:1.

Both errors had already shipped into DESIGN.md, the aggregate, the wiki page,
and a priority-6 radio message to the root for bridge's implementer. All four
surfaces were corrected, a superseded-finding note was added at the head of
the child's study so a direct reader is not misled, and a correction went out
(31BA28A8).

One thing the correction improved rather than merely fixed: because the
harness writes summary *text* into the transcript and `compactMetadata`
carries preserved-message UUID lists, the "summary hash, never summary text"
rule stopped being a theoretical §6.1 guard and became a live one. Bridge
condition 3 is now explicitly extended — append-only structured metadata is
readable, conversational structure and the summary body are not.

### Note for the next run

The generalizable rule, now in memory: **a child's negative existence claim
("no X exists") is a premise, not a finding, and gets verified before any
ruling rests on it.** The check cost one script; the error had already
propagated to four surfaces and another node's implementer. Positive findings
a child reports are cheap to trust because they cite evidence; negative ones
assert the absence of evidence, and absence is exactly what a narrow search
produces by accident.
EOF
echo appended

attachment.snippet:
1	# 35.1 Evergreen Synthesis And Model Policy
2	
3	All three research children have settled and merged. This run is synthesis and
4	adjudication — no new research. Four decisions land, each with a decision-log
5	row naming its requester and verdict.
6	
7	## Delegation decision (stated out loud, per the rule)
8	
9	**No children this run.** Checked against the three triggers:
10	
11	- *Separable subtask with real depth* — no. The remaining work is synthesis of
12	  research already delivered, plus manifest authoring. My contract says I
13	  synthesize and never forward raw child output up the tree; delegating the
14	  aggregate would be delegating the one thing that is definitionally mine.
15	- *Independent parallel subtasks* — no. The four decisions below are coupled:
16	  the compaction ruling constrains the evergreen v1 line (§5 continuity), the
17	  model-policy correction and the inventory's stale tier table are the same
18	  defect, and the Buzz cross-post set depends on the v1 read/write boundary.
19	- *Clean context* — no. The long source material is already read and
20	  compressed here; a fresh context would have to re-read all 975 lines to do
21	  worse.
22	
23	Spawning now would be spawning for a sub-iteration chore — the named failure
24	mode. Budget ($14.5 remaining) is ample for solo synthesis.
25	
26	## Decision 1 — Model-policy retiering (requester: root, directive A6BE089F)
27	
28	**Verdict: approve.** Touches no review-bar principle; it tunes an economic
29	parameter. Early read already sent (05237766); the row and the manifest edits
30	land this run.
31	
32	Manifest consequences:
33	
34	1. `docs/DESIGN.md:108` names tiers literally ("haiku-develop/fable-review
35	   model policy") — stale against the live tree. Rewrite to the **shape
36	   invariant** (orchestrator writes precise work orders → cheaper tier
37	   executes → stronger tier reviews) and record the current assignment as a
38	   tunable parameter. The next retiering then becomes a parameter edit, not a
39	   manifest contradiction.
40	2. §3: template versions are registry-backed **today** — dev-node v2
41	   registered as a signed 42050 (version event `0f8d0910`, pin `c6696b7`), the
42	   registry's first real version bump. §3 currently describes this as future
43	   ("hand-rolled in git until `registry` holds template versions").
44	3. §7 economics: the decomposition doctrine's claim is a *ratio* (execution
45	   cheaper than review and orchestration), which sonnet-develop/opus-review
46	   preserves — so the doctrine stands. What shifts is absolute per-child burn,
47	   which makes haiku-era cap-sizing guidance stale. Flag it; do not invent
48	   replacement numbers (none observed yet at the new tiers).
49	
50	Corroboration found in the research: `inventory.md` requirement 12 records the
51	tier table as "Orchestrator:Fable, Review:Fable, Development:Haiku" — the same
52	staleness, independently. That is the argument for the generator reading tiers
53	from a source rather than restating them.
54	
55	## Decision 2 — Compaction-to-task mapping (requester: root, directive 7803C28A; §8 → §5)
56	
57	The child recommended Candidate A (a distinct compaction event, task
58	coordinates in tags, `e`-tag `summary-of` marker, metrics-only content).
59	**Adopt the shape; reject one of its four next-steps; add a detection ruling
60	it did not reach.**
61	
62	- **Adopt** Candidate A's shape. Signed, discoverable, privacy-tight, and
63	  harvestable inside the one adapter (bridge condition 3, verdict 8266A685).
64	- **Reject next-step 3** — "coordinate with Claude Code's harness to emit a
65	  transcript record type `compaction`". A design surface may not depend on an
66	  unshipped change to an external platform we do not control. Direct
67	  precedent: decision row 313 endorsed the buzz kind-allowlist PR as a
68	  parallel track while ruling that *no design surface may depend on its
69	  acceptance*; §6.3 is the same instinct applied to Fractal. The
70	  recommendation as written is contingent on a marker that does not exist, so
71	  it is unbuildable.
72	- **Detection ruling (mine, not the child's).** Compaction is already
73	  observable in the data the harvester reads today: within one session the
74	  per-request context size (`input_tokens` + `cache_read_input_tokens`) grows
75	  monotonically and *resets sharply* at a compaction. So compaction is
76	  detectable as a **usage-series discontinuity** — no new transcript record
77	  type, no upstream dependency, no conversational parsing (honoring bridge
78	  condition 3's "append-only per-request usage fields only"), and it lands in
79	  exactly one module (constraint b).
80	- **Provenance grade on the event.** Because detection is an inference rather
81	  than an attested fact, the event carries a `detection` tag —
82	  `usage-discontinuity` today, `harness-marker` if one ever ships. A reader
83	  can then tell a derived claim from an attested one. This is §6.5's
84	  verify-don't-assume posture applied to our own telemetry.
85	- **Kind allocation: 42060.** State the convention that makes it mechanical:
86	  the 420xx regular block is decade-per-family (42010 lifecycle, 42020
87	  accounting, 42030 digest, 42040/42041 approval, 42050 template), with
88	  intra-family increments in the units digit. Session compaction is its own
89	  family, so it takes the next decade. *Condition:* `core` re-runs the
90	  block/buzz collision check before writing `docs/kinds/42060-*.md` (nearest
91	  Buzz neighbour was 42000 @ 06e3d82; 42060 is expected clear, but the check
92	  is core's to run and mine to require).
93	- **§6.1 compliance:** content carries metrics and a **summary hash, never
94	  summary text**; tags carry task coordinates only. The pointer resolves to
95	  the node's *own* step event, so a child's compaction event stays in its
96	  subgraph — only digests roll up.
97	
98	This **closes** the §8 open question: it moves from §8 into §5 as current
99	truth, with the decision-log row carrying the reasoning.
100	
101	## Decision 3 — What evergreen IS at v1 (requester: root, directive 7803C28A; §5)
102	
103	The inventory classified 28 requirements: 16 relay-derivable, 7
104	fractal-derivable, 4 human-authored, 1 mixed. The three framings on the table
105	were generator / query-CLI / both.
106	
107	**Ruling: evergreen v1 is the read plane; Fractal's own CLI remains the write
108	plane.** Concretely, v1 is (a) a query surface over the signed log and (b) a
109	generator/maintainer of the standing context surface. It ships no write path.
110	
111	Reasoning to record:
112	
113	- Every write-side gap the inventory names — radio send UI, signal API, live
114	  NODE.md edit UI, node-spawn bridge — is a wrapper around a Fractal
115	  capability that already exists and works. Wrapping adds a second control
116	  path with no new capability, and an "orchestration layer bridging Evergreen
117	  to Fractal's node-spawn API" drifts straight at principle §6.3. The correct
118	  answer to "signal routing is Fractal CLI only" is: *that is Fractal's job.*
119	- The read side is where nothing exists and everything is needed: 16 of 28
120	  requirements are relay-derivable with, per the inventory, no query surface
121	  at all.
122	- §6.2 falls out clean: v1 reconstructs from relay + Fractal SQLite, holds no
123	  local index, and thereby extends the relay-as-cursor (bridge) and
124	  relay-as-registry (registry) precedents to **relay-as-context**.
125	
126	v1 line through §5's four capabilities:
127	
128	| Capability | v1 ships (read) | Stays Fractal CLI (write) |
129	|---|---|---|
130	| Commission trees | template catalog: versions, history, instance linkage | the spawn itself |
131	| Steer subgraphs | situational awareness: aggregates, budgets, pending gates | radio send, signals, live edits |
132	| Review approvals | the queue: 42040 pending, 42041 verdicts | the verdict (a radio reply) |
133	| Query own history | **owned fully — this *is* the capability** | — |
134	
135	The generated context surface is a composite: a human-authored preamble
136	(mission, phase, non-negotiables, governance mode, pointers — the 4

content.text:
REVIEW found a real defect, and it was in the most consequential claim I shipped.

**The load-bearing claim was false.** My compaction ruling rested on the child's finding that no compaction marker exists in transcripts. Verifying that premise — one script over `transcripts/` — showed the harness *already* writes a `system` record with `compactMetadata` (`trigger`, `preTokens`, `postTokens`, `durationMs`, `cumulativeDroppedTokens`, preserved-segment ids), plus a `user` record flagged `isCompactSummary`. Two markers across 36 transcripts, in exactly the two sessions that compacted. My reasoning about rejecting the upstream dependency was sound — and solving a problem that didn't exist. `preTokens`/`postTokens` *are* the metrics 42060 reports, so detection is now attested rather than inferred.

**The substitute I invented was independently wrong.** The two-term series I published (`input_tokens` + `cache_read_input_tokens`) tracks cache turnover, not context: cache expiry collapses `cache_read` while `cache_creation` absorbs the prompt. Measured across the four largest sessions it fired 4–6 false positives each. The three-term form fired twice — in exactly the two marker-bearing sessions. 1:1. It survives only as a documented fallback, in corrected form.

Both errors had already shipped to four surfaces *and* a priority-6 message to root for bridge's implementer. Corrected all of them: DESIGN.md §5.2 and its decision-log row, the research aggregate (which now states plainly where the study was wrong), the wiki page, and a superseded-finding note at the head of the child's study so a direct reader isn't misled. Correction sent to root (31BA28A8).

The correction also *improved* the design rather than just repairing it: because the harness writes summary text into the transcript and `compactMetadata` carries preserved-message UUID lists, "summary hash, never summary text" went from a theoretical §6.1 guard to a live one. Bridge condition 3 is now explicitly extended — append-only structured metadata readable, conversational structure and the summary body not.

Memory pruned to terminal state with the generalizable rule: **a child's negative existence claim is a premise, not a finding.** A positive finding cites evidence; a negative one asserts absence, which is exactly what a narrow search produces by accident. Post-mortem appended to the plan.

Both stores lint clean (only the mechanical `_index.md` notes), `test.sh` exits 0, tree is in scope for COMMIT.
